headline: GLICs Hold RM1.8 Trillion But Coordination Between 7 Funds Is Minimal
context: Malaysia's 7 Government-Linked Investment Companies collectively manage RM1.8 trillion in assets — equivalent to 112% of GDP — but operate with minimal coordination, leading to overlapping investments, conflicting objectives, and suboptimal national returns.

cards.big: RM1.8 trillion across 7 funds — EPF, KWAP, Khazanah, PNB, LTH, LTAT, and Kumpulan Wang Persaraan collectively larger than GDP
cards.sub: 7 funds often compete against each other for the same assets — driving up acquisition prices and reducing returns for all

cards.big: Overlapping investments: 4 GLICs are simultaneously top-10 shareholders in 18 Bursa-listed companies
cards.sub: Competitive bidding between GLICs for the same assets cost an estimated RM2.4 billion in inflated acquisition prices since 2020

cards.big: EPF serves 15 million members, PNB serves 14 million — combined 29 million beneficiaries represent nearly all Malaysian adults
cards.sub: Returns affect every household yet no coordinated investment strategy ensures national interest alignment

cards.big: Putrajaya Committee on GLC High Performance meets quarterly but has no binding authority over individual GLIC investment decisions
cards.sub: Each GLIC reports to a different minister — Finance, PM's office, Defence — creating structural incoherence

cards.big: 7 sovereign funds managing RM1.8 trillion without coordination is like 7 captains steering one ship in different directions
Should Malaysia establish a National Investment Council with authority to coordinate GLIC strategies and prevent competitive overlap?
